repairBuyOrReplace.h2: CLK270 / CLK320 CDI — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Essential maintenance — catch before injectors seize
Replace low‑pressure and high‑pressure pumps if damaged
Essential maintenance — catch before catastrophic failure
Piston‑valve contact means little is left to repair
Labour‑intensive but essential — prevent oil loss

buyingChecks.title: Buying This CLK CDI? Check These First
buyIf: * CLK270 (2002–2005) with injector seals recently replaced (no black death residue)
* CLK320 (2005–2009) with timing chain history (post‑2010 or chain replaced)
* Full service history with regular oil changes — both engines are sensitive to oil quality
* No cold‑start rattling (timing chain health — OM642)
* No oil leaks around the V of the engine (oil cooler seal health — OM642)
* Swirl flap and EGR system recently serviced
avoidIf: * Black carbon deposits around injectors — injector seal leaks ("black death") are developing (OM612)
* Cold‑start rattling and no timing chain history — budget for chain work (OM642)
* Oil loss or burning oil smell — oil cooler seals may be failing (OM642)
* No service history — both engines are unforgiving of neglect
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis — may indicate timing, injector or EGR problems

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The CLK270 and CLK320 CDI are Mercedes‑Benz's stylish diesel coupés and cabriolets from the C209/A209 generation. The CLK270 (2002–2005) uses the OM612 2.7‑litre inline‑five — a robust engine with a weakness for injector seal leaks ("black death") and fuel pump issues. The CLK320 (2005–2009) uses the OM642 3.0‑litre V6 — a more powerful and refined engine with 224 bhp and 510 Nm torque, but with well‑documented vulnerabilities: timing chain stretch on pre‑2010 units, oil cooler seal leaks, swirl flap rod breakage, and exhaust manifold cracks. With typical CLK values ranging from £3,000 to £10,000, an engine replacement is a significant investment — a well‑maintained example with documented maintenance history is the safest choice. The CLK320 offers significantly more performance, but the OM642 requires proactive attention to its known weaknesses.

cta.label: → Compare CLK CDI engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes‑Benz CLK270 / CLK320 CDI (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Injector Seals — Inspect for black carbon deposits around injectors ("black death" warning — OM612) — see the map above
* Cold Start — Listen for rattling or clattering (timing chain tensioner warning — OM642 particularly)
* Service History — Including which engine era (OM612 or OM642) and oil‑change discipline
* Timing Chain — For OM642 (CLK320): check for any chain work or cold‑start rattle
* Oil Leaks — Check around the V of the engine for oil cooler seal leaks (OM642)
* Swirl Flap / EGR — Check for smooth idle and acceleration; any roughness may indicate issues (OM642)
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes read; unresolved issues may indicate timing, injector, EGR or sensor problems
* MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures

items.question: How much does a Mercedes‑Benz CLK270 / CLK320 CDI engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned CLK CDI engine typically costs £3,500–£5,500 supply only, with the OM642 3.0 V6 variant at the higher end. Fitting adds £800–£1,300 depending on the specialist. A used OM642.910 engine costs on average £2,638. A reconditioned engine fully installed typically costs around £1,800–£2,800. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your CLK‑Class.

items.question: Is the Mercedes‑Benz CLK270 / CLK320 CDI engine reliable?
items.answer: It depends on which engine your CLK has. The CLK270 (2002–2005) uses the OM612 2.7‑litre inline‑five — a robust engine with a weakness for injector seal leaks ("black death") and fuel pump issues. The CLK320 (2005–2009) uses the OM642 3.0‑litre V6 — a more powerful engine with 224 bhp, but with well‑documented vulnerabilities: timing chain stretch on pre‑2010 units, oil cooler seal leaks, swirl flap rod breakage, and exhaust manifold cracks. With proper maintenance and proactive attention to known issues, both can be reliable.

items.question: What are the common problems with the Mercedes‑Benz CLK270 / CLK320 CDI?
items.answer: CLK270 (OM612): Injector seal leaks ("black death" — soot and carbon buildup around injectors causing a chuffing noise), low‑pressure and high‑pressure fuel pump failure, and injector failure. CLK320 (OM642): Timing chain stretch on pre‑2010 units (rattling on cold start), oil cooler seal leaks (oil loss in the V of the engine), swirl flap rod breakage (rough idle, loss of power), EGR valve clogging, and exhaust manifold cracks.

items.question: Is it worth replacing a CLK CDI engine, or should I scrap the car?
items.answer: Depends on the vehicle value and condition. Against a well‑kept CLK320 worth £5,000–£10,000, a £5,000–£6,000 recon is 50–100% of the car's value — borderline but often worth it for a cherished example. Against a tired CLK270 with significant body issues, the same recon may exceed our 70% Mercedes‑Benz threshold — get a scrap valuation first. The CLK320 commands a premium due to its V6 performance and refinement.

items.question: How do I find my Mercedes‑Benz CLK270 / CLK320 CDI engine code?
items.answer: CLK270 CDI (2002–2005): OM612.962 or OM612.967. CLK320 CDI (2005–2009): OM642.910. Check the VIN plate or vehicle documentation. The engine code is also stamped on the engine block. The CLK270 produces 170 bhp; the CLK320 produces 224 bhp.

items.question: Can I repair the OM642 timing chain, or does the engine need replacement?
items.answer: The timing chain and tensioner must be replaced if worn. A preventative replacement costs £1,500–£3,500. A snapped chain almost always destroys the engine, requiring a £4,500–£7,500+ replacement. At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, book an inspection immediately. Regular oil changes are the key to preventing timing chain issues.
